sections.title: Five Skills Tested
items: Noting Details — Who, What, When, Where, Why, How
Extent of Vocabulary — Understanding unfamiliar words from context
Identifying the Main Idea — Topic sentence or central theme
Inferring Details and Predicting Results — Going beyond what is explicitly stated
Tone/Attitude — Sensing the author's mood

sections.title: Types of Questions
rows: What is the article about? / The passage is primarily concerned with…
According to the author… / The author states the following except…
The author implies that… / It is likely that…
Which best describes the tone? / The author's tone is…
The word '____' is used to denote… / The term '____' means…

sections.title: Reading Tips
items: Read the paragraph before the questions.
Try to read fast but do not skip lines.
While reading, anticipate what the passage is about.
Always verify your answer by going back to the passage.
The main idea is usually in the first or last sentence.
Watch for too specific/broad titles — the right title covers everything in the passage.
For specific details questions, choose options directly stated in the paragraph.
For inference questions, choose options that logically develop from the information.
For mood/tone questions, look for emotion words (great, brutal, greedy, arrogant, etc.).
Use context clues for unfamiliar words.

sections.title: Paragraph Organization Techniques
items: Identify the Topic Sentence — It is usually broad and is supported by all other sentences.
Note Recurring Words and Phrases — Writers repeat words/phrases to establish one main topic.
Look Out for Transitions — Conjunctions and transitional words (for example, however, therefore, first, next, lastly) connect sentences and show relationships.
Spatial arrangement — Left to right, top to bottom, near to far.
Chronological arrangement — According to when events happened (first, second, next, then, lastly).
Topical arrangement — Ascending or descending order of importance.
Determine the Concluding Sentence — Sums up all the points; usually found at the end.

sections.title: Paragraph Organization Patterns
rows: Presentation → Looks from afar → Close-up view → Other features → Concluding impression
Principle → Relevant facts/evidence → Restatement of principle
Introduction of subject → Principal features → Other relevant points → Summary
